clean_story:
Metals buried in planets. Leftovers from cosmic forges.

The term planetary metal deposits refers to concentrations of iron, nickel, gold, or platinum trapped in a planet’s crust or core—remnants of asteroid impacts, volcanic eruptions, or the death of ancient stars.

Most metals didn’t form on Earth. They arrived as debris from collisions 4.5 billion years ago, like a delivery truck dumping scrap into a planet’s molten surface. Heavy metals (iron, nickel) sank to the core; lighter ones (gold, copper) stayed near the crust. Some deposits are pure accidents: A single asteroid strike in Sudbury, Canada, left a 60-km crater—and $10 trillion worth of nickel and copper.

Volcanoes act like metal refineries. Magma dissolves metals deep underground, then deposits them in cracks as it cools, forming veins richer than any human mine. The Bushveld Complex in South Africa holds 80% of the world’s platinum this way—a 2-billion-year-old slow-cooker of molten rock.

Three related subjects are asteroid mining economics, supervolcano metal vents, and how stars forge heavy elements.
